lessons.subject: Social Sciences
lessons.topic: Geography: map skills and local places
lessons.subtopic: Geography: map skills and local places
lessons.title: Grade 6 Geography: map skills and local places — Core teaching
learning_objectives: Explain core concepts related to geography: map skills and local places.
Use CAPS enquiry, practical, creative or source-work skills for the topic.
Apply the topic in familiar South African classroom and everyday contexts.
lessons.lesson_body: In this lesson learners study geography: map skills and local places using clear classroom examples. CAPS focus: Explain core concepts related to geography: map skills and local places. Key vocabulary: Geography:, skills, local, places. Use the map, source or timeline provided and explain how the evidence supports the answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 6.SS.1.1 covers Geography: map skills and local places. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.
lessons.explanation: In this lesson learners study geography: map skills and local places using clear classroom examples. CAPS focus: Explain core concepts related to geography: map skills and local places. Key vocabulary: Geography:, skills, local, places. Use the map, source or timeline provided and explain how the evidence supports the answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 6.SS.1.1 covers Geography: map skills and local places. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: A map shows two towns linked by a river near geography: map skills and local places. Which source would best explain why settlers chose the site?
step_by_step_solution: List the physical features shown on the map.
Match each feature to a human need such as water or transport.
Choose the source that explains settlement reasons.
Write one sentence using evidence from the source.
worked_examples.answer: A historical source describing water access and farming explains the settlement choice.

worked_examples.question: Place these events about geography: map skills and local places in the correct order: policy change, protest march, community meeting.
step_by_step_solution: Identify which event happened first.
Decide which event caused the next event.
Arrange all events chronologically.
Check the order against the source summary.
worked_examples.answer: Community meeting, protest march, policy change.

practice_questions.question_text: Which source would best answer a question about geography: map skills and local places in the local area?

options.A: A fictional story with no historical details
options.B: A community interview or local archive record
options.C: A picture with no caption or date

practice_questions.explanation: Local sources provide relevant evidence.

practice_questions.question_text: When comparing places linked to geography: map skills and local places, what should learners examine first?

options.A: Only the colours used on the map
options.B: The title of the textbook page
options.C: Physical features and human activities shown on the map
options.D: The number of pages in the atlas

practice_questions.explanation: Maps show features and activities that explain differences.

practice_questions.question_text: Which timeline order is logical for an event about geography: map skills and local places?

options.A: Consequence, cause, event
options.B: Event, event, event with no order
options.C: Random order with no dates

practice_questions.explanation: Timelines show cause and effect over time.

answer_key.correct_answer_text: A community interview or local archive record
Physical features and human activities shown on the map
Cause, event, consequence

remediation_hints.hint_text: When topic vocabulary confusion appears, return to the worked example for geography: map skills and local places and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Highlight the date, place and source before answering.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 6 example about geography: map skills and local places and teach it to a partner using the standard approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 6.SS.1.1 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect geography: map skills and local places to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for geography: map skills and local places. Use the standard variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: social sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about geography: map skills and local places without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports social sciences learning at home.

learning_objectives: Explain core concepts related to geography: physical features and resources.
Use CAPS enquiry, practical, creative or source-work skills for the topic.
Apply the topic in familiar South African classroom and everyday contexts.
lessons.lesson_body: This visual lesson helps learners see geography: physical features and resources with diagrams, models and labelled drawings. CAPS focus: Explain core concepts related to geography: physical features and resources. Key vocabulary: Geography:, physical, features, resources. Use the map, source or timeline provided and explain how the evidence supports the answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 6.SS.2.1 covers Geography: physical features and resources. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.
lessons.explanation: This visual lesson helps learners see geography: physical features and resources with diagrams, models and labelled drawings. CAPS focus: Explain core concepts related to geography: physical features and resources. Key vocabulary: Geography:, physical, features, resources. Use the map, source or timeline provided and explain how the evidence supports the answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 6.SS.2.1 covers Geography: physical features and resources. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: A map shows two towns linked by a river near geography: physical features and resources. Which source would best explain why settlers chose the site?
step_by_step_solution: List the physical features shown on the map.
Match each feature to a human need such as water or transport.
Choose the source that explains settlement reasons.
Write one sentence using evidence from the source.
worked_examples.answer: A historical source describing water access and farming explains the settlement choice.

worked_examples.question: Place these events about geography: physical features and resources in the correct order: policy change, protest march, community meeting.
step_by_step_solution: Identify which event happened first.
Decide which event caused the next event.
Arrange all events chronologically.
Check the order against the source summary.
worked_examples.answer: Community meeting, protest march, policy change.

practice_questions.question_text: Which source would best answer a question about geography: physical features and resources in the local area?

options.A: A fictional story with no historical details
options.B: A picture with no caption or date
options.C: A community interview or local archive record

practice_questions.explanation: Local sources provide relevant evidence.

practice_questions.question_text: When comparing places linked to geography: physical features and resources, what should learners examine first?

options.A: Only the colours used on the map
options.B: The title of the textbook page
options.C: The number of pages in the atlas
options.D: Physical features and human activities shown on the map

practice_questions.explanation: Maps show features and activities that explain differences.

practice_questions.question_text: Which timeline order is logical for an event about geography: physical features and resources?

options.A: Cause, event, consequence
options.B: Consequence, cause, event
options.C: Event, event, event with no order

practice_questions.explanation: Timelines show cause and effect over time.

answer_key.correct_answer_text: A community interview or local archive record
Physical features and human activities shown on the map
Cause, event, consequence

remediation_hints.hint_text: When procedure without conceptual understanding appears, return to the worked example for geography: physical features and resources and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Highlight the date, place and source before answering.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 6 example about geography: physical features and resources and teach it to a partner using the visual approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 6.SS.2.1 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect geography: physical features and resources to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for geography: physical features and resources. Use the visual variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: social sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about geography: physical features and resources without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports social sciences learning at home.

source_citations.citation_text: CAPS reference 6.SS.2.1 covers Geography: physical features and resources.

lessons.lesson_body: In this lesson learners study history: societies and change using clear classroom examples. CAPS focus: Explain core concepts related to history: societies and change. Key vocabulary: History:, societies, change. Use the map, source or timeline provided and explain how the evidence supports the answer. Grounding snippet: CAPS reference 6.SS.2.2 covers History: societies and change. Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: A map shows two towns linked by a river near history: societies and change. Which source would best explain why settlers chose the site?
step_by_step_solution: List the physical features shown on the map.
Match each feature to a human need such as water or transport.
Choose the source that explains settlement reasons.
Write one sentence using evidence from the source.
worked_examples.answer: A historical source describing water access and farming explains the settlement choice.

worked_examples.question: Place these events about history: societies and change in the correct order: policy change, protest march, community meeting.
step_by_step_solution: Identify which event happened first.
Decide which event caused the next event.
Arrange all events chronologically.
Check the order against the source summary.
worked_examples.answer: Community meeting, protest march, policy change.

practice_questions.question_text: Which source would best answer a question about history: societies and change in the local area?

options.A: A fictional story with no historical details
options.B: A community interview or local archive record
options.C: A picture with no caption or date

practice_questions.explanation: Local sources provide relevant evidence.

practice_questions.question_text: When comparing places linked to history: societies and change, what should learners examine first?

options.A: Only the colours used on the map
options.B: The title of the textbook page
options.C: Physical features and human activities shown on the map
options.D: The number of pages in the atlas

practice_questions.explanation: Maps show features and activities that explain differences.

practice_questions.question_text: Which timeline order is logical for an event about history: societies and change?

options.A: Consequence, cause, event
options.B: Event, event, event with no order
options.C: Random order with no dates

practice_questions.explanation: Timelines show cause and effect over time.

answer_key.correct_answer_text: A community interview or local archive record
Physical features and human activities shown on the map
Cause, event, consequence

remediation_hints.example: Highlight the date, place and source before answering.

Key vocabulary: Assessment, MTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMT, MTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMT, MTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMT. Use the map, source or timeline provided and explain how the evidence supports the answer. Grounding snippet: --- page 1 ---
/MT66/MT97/MT115/MT105/MT99/MT32/MT69/MT100/MT117/MT99/MT97/MT116/MT105/MT111/MT110
/MT68/MT101/MT112/MT97/MT114/MT116/MT109/MT101/MT110/MT116/MT58
/MT82/MT69/MT80/MT85/MT66/MT76/MT73/MT67/MT32/MT79/MT70/M Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: A map shows two towns linked by a river near assessment. Which source would best explain why settlers chose the site?
step_by_step_solution: List the physical features shown on the map.
Match each feature to a human need such as water or transport.
Choose the source that explains settlement reasons.
Write one sentence using evidence from the source.
worked_examples.answer: A historical source describing water access and farming explains the settlement choice.

worked_examples.question: Place these events about assessment in the correct order: policy change, protest march, community meeting.
step_by_step_solution: Identify which event happened first.
Decide which event caused the next event.
Arrange all events chronologically.
Check the order against the source summary.
worked_examples.answer: Community meeting, protest march, policy change.

practice_questions.question_text: Which source would best answer a question about assessment in the local area?

options.A: A fictional story with no historical details
options.B: A community interview or local archive record
options.C: A picture with no caption or date

practice_questions.explanation: Local sources provide relevant evidence.

practice_questions.question_text: When comparing places linked to assessment, what should learners examine first?

options.A: Only the colours used on the map
options.B: The title of the textbook page
options.C: Physical features and human activities shown on the map
options.D: The number of pages in the atlas

practice_questions.explanation: Maps show features and activities that explain differences.

practice_questions.question_text: Which timeline order is logical for an event about assessment?

options.A: Consequence, cause, event
options.B: Event, event, event with no order
options.C: Random order with no dates

practice_questions.explanation: Timelines show cause and effect over time.

answer_key.correct_answer_text: A community interview or local archive record
Physical features and human activities shown on the map
Cause, event, consequence

remediation_hints.hint_text: When topic vocabulary confusion appears, return to the worked example for assessment and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Highlight the date, place and source before answering.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 6 example about assessment and teach it to a partner using the real world sa approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 6.SS.2.4 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect assessment to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for assessment. Use the real world sa variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: social sciences.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about assessment without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports social sciences learning at home.

worked_examples.question: A map shows two towns linked by a river near assessment. Which source would best explain why settlers chose the site?
step_by_step_solution: List the physical features shown on the map.
Match each feature to a human need such as water or transport.
Choose the source that explains settlement reasons.
Write one sentence using evidence from the source.
worked_examples.answer: A historical source describing water access and farming explains the settlement choice.

worked_examples.question: Place these events about assessment in the correct order: policy change, protest march, community meeting.
step_by_step_solution: Identify which event happened first.
Decide which event caused the next event.
Arrange all events chronologically.
Check the order against the source summary.
worked_examples.answer: Community meeting, protest march, policy change.

practice_questions.question_text: Which source would best answer a question about assessment in the local area?

options.A: A community interview or local archive record
options.B: A fictional story with no historical details
options.C: A picture with no caption or date

practice_questions.explanation: Local sources provide relevant evidence.

practice_questions.question_text: When comparing places linked to assessment, what should learners examine first?

options.A: Only the colours used on the map
options.B: Physical features and human activities shown on the map
options.C: The title of the textbook page
options.D: The number of pages in the atlas

practice_questions.explanation: Maps show features and activities that explain differences.

practice_questions.question_text: Which timeline order is logical for an event about assessment?

options.A: Consequence, cause, event
options.B: Event, event, event with no order
options.C: Cause, event, consequence

practice_questions.explanation: Timelines show cause and effect over time.

answer_key.correct_answer_text: A community interview or local archive record
Physical features and human activities shown on the map
Cause, event, consequence
